5.3 Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity>Heat capacity
5.5 Change of phase>Latent heat of vaporization

distribution_analysis.least_covered_section: 5.3 Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the latent heat of vaporization?

options.A: Heat required to raise 1 kg of liquid by 1 K
options.B: Heat required to change 1 kg of solid to liquid at constant temperature
options.C: Heat required to change 1 kg of liquid to gas at constant temperature
options.D: Heat required to change 1 kg of gas to solid

questions.explanation: Latent heat of vaporization \(L_v\) is the heat absorbed by 1 kg of a substance to change from liquid to gas at constant temperature (boiling point). Option B describes latent heat of fusion.

source_trace.content_anchor: amount of heat absorbed by 1kg of the substance to change its phase from liquid to gas

explanations_tiered.definition: Latent heat of vaporization is the energy required to convert 1 kg of liquid to gas at constant temperature.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For water at 100°C, \(L_v = 2.26 \times 10^6\,\mathrm{J/kg}\), much larger than \(L_f\) because bonds must be completely broken.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Boiling water needs extra heat to turn into steam, even though the temperature stays at 100°C — that's latent heat of vaporization.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: During vaporization, the heat supplied breaks intermolecular bonds, increasing potential energy but not kinetic energy, so temperature is constant.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The latent heat of vaporization is related to the enthalpy of vaporization: \(\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = mL_v\). For water at 100°C, \(\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 40.7\,\mathrm{kJ/mol}\).
reasoning_steps: Latent heat means no temperature change
misconceptions: Some think boiling water gets hotter if you keep heating it; it does not — temperature stays at boiling point until all liquid vaporises.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: L_v: liquid → gas, constant T. Water: L_v = 2.26×10⁶ J/kg.

glossary_entry.definition: Heat to convert 1 kg of liquid to gas at constant temperature.

flashcard.front: What is latent heat of vaporization?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Heat required to change 1 kg of liquid to gas at constant temperature.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Latent heat of vaporization: heat to change 1 kg liquid → gas at constant T.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Heat to vaporise 1 kg of liquid at constant temperature.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: L_v: liquid → gas, constant T. Much larger than L_f.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare L_v for water (2.26×10⁶ J/kg) with L_f (3.33×10⁵ J/kg).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Latent heat of vaporization is for liquid → gas at constant temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.structural_anchor: Measurement of specific latent heat of steam
questions.question: Steam at 100°C is passed into 0.250 kg of water at 20.0°C in a calorimeter. The final temperature is 40.0°C. If the latent heat of vaporization of water is \(2.26 \times 10^6\,\mathrm{J/kg}\), what mass of steam condensed? (Specific heat of water = 4186 J/(kg·K))
questions.hint: Heat lost by steam (condensation + cooling condensed water) = heat gained by cold water.
questions.explanation: Let m_s be mass of steam. Heat gained by cold water = \(0.250 \times 4186 \times (40.0 - 20.0) = 0.250 \times 4186 \times 20 = 20930\,\mathrm{J}\). Heat lost by steam = \(m_s L_v + m_s c_w (100 - 40) = m_s (2.26\times10^6 + 4186\times60) = m_s (2.26\times10^6 + 251160) = m_s (2.51116\times10^6)\). Equate: \(m_s = 20930 / 2.51116\times10^6 = 0.00833\,\mathrm{kg} = 8.33\times10^{-3}\,\mathrm{kg}\), closest to 8.7×10⁻³ kg.
formula_used: \(m_s L_v + m_s c_w (T_{\text{steam}} - T_f) = m_w c_w (T_f - T_{\text{cold}})\)

calculation_steps: Heat gained by cold water = 0.250 × 4186 × 20 = 20930 J
Heat lost by steam = m_s × (2.26×10⁶ + 4186 × 60) = m_s × 2.511×10⁶

source_trace.structural_element: Measurement of specific latent heat of steam
source_trace.content_anchor: m_s L_v + m_s c_w (T_s - T_c) = m_w c_w (T_c - T_w) + m_c c_c (T_c - T_w)

explanations_tiered.definition: When steam condenses, it releases latent heat of vaporisation; the condensed water then cools to the final temperature.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The heat balance is: heat to warm cold water = heat from condensing steam + heat from cooling condensed steam from 100°C to T_f.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Steam gives off a lot of heat when it turns to water, then the hot water cools down. That heat warms the cold water.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Set \(m_w c_w (T_f - T_c) = m_s L_v + m_s c_w (T_s - T_f)\) and solve for \(m_s\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The calorimeter's heat capacity should be included for accuracy: \(m_w c_w (T_f - T_c) + C_{\text{cal}}(T_f - T_c) = m_s L_v + m_s c_w (T_s - T_f)\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Heat gained by cold water = 0.250 × 4186 × 20 = 20930 J.
Step 2: Heat lost by steam condensation = m_s × 2.26×10⁶ J.
Step 3: Heat lost by cooling condensed water = m_s × 4186 × 60 = m_s × 251160 J.
Step 4: Total heat lost = m_s(2.26×10⁶ + 251160) = m_s × 2.511×10⁶ J.
reasoning_steps: Calculate heat gained by cold water
Express heat lost by steam (condensation + cooling)
Set equal and solve for m_s

formula_analysis.formula: \(m_s L_v + m_s c_w (T_s - T_f) = m_w c_w (T_f - T_c)\)
formula_analysis.name: Steam calorimetry

misconceptions: Some forget to include the cooling of condensed water from 100°C to T_f, underestimating the heat lost by steam.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Steam calorimetry: Q_steam = m_s L_v + m_s c_w (100 - T_f).

glossary_entry.definition: Measuring latent heat of vaporization using steam condensation.

flashcard.front: How to find mass of steam condensed in calorimeter?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Use m_s L_v + m_s c_w (100 - T_f) = m_w c_w (T_f - T_c).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Heat gained by water = m_s L_v + m_s c_w (100 - T_f).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Method to measure L_v using steam condensation.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Q_steam = m_s L_v + m_s c_w ΔT_cool.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that steam releases heat both during condensation and cooling.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why does a metal feel colder than a piece of wood at the same room temperature?

options.A: Metal has a lower temperature than wood
options.B: Metal is a better conductor of heat
options.C: Metal has a higher specific heat capacity

questions.hint: Consider how quickly each material conducts heat away from your hand.
questions.explanation: Metal is a good thermal conductor, so it rapidly conducts heat away from your hand, making your hand feel colder. Wood is an insulator, so heat transfer is slow, and your hand loses less heat, feeling warmer.

source_trace.content_anchor: Why does the metal feel colder than the wood if they are both at room temperature?

explanations_tiered.definition: Thermal conductivity \(k\) determines how fast heat flows through a material.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Metals have high thermal conductivity, so they transfer heat away from your hand quickly. Wood has low thermal conductivity, so it feels warmer because heat transfer is slow.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Metal steals heat from your hand faster than wood, so your hand feels colder even though they are the same temperature.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The rate of heat transfer is \(P = kA\frac{\Delta T}{L}\). Metal has high \(k\), so heat flows rapidly from your hand, giving a sensation of cold.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The skin's temperature receptors detect the rate of heat loss, not absolute temperature. High thermal conductivity materials cause rapid heat loss, perceived as cold.
reasoning_steps: Both objects are at same temperature
Faster heat loss from hand → colder sensation
misconceptions: Many believe metal is actually colder than wood. At room temperature, they are the same temperature; the sensation is due to different heat transfer rates.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Metal feels cold because it conducts heat away from skin rapidly.

glossary_entry.definition: Measure of a material's ability to conduct heat.

flashcard.front: Why does metal feel colder than wood at room temperature?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Metal is a better conductor, so it transfers heat away from your hand faster.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Metal has high thermal conductivity, so it draws heat from your hand quickly.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: High k → feels cold. Low k → feels warm (insulator).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a metal spoon and wooden spoon in hot water.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain heat conduction in everyday situations
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Metal is a better conductor, so it transfers heat away from the hand faster, creating a sensation of cold.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A steel wire has a length of 5.000 m at 20.0°C. The coefficient of linear expansion of steel is \(1.2 \times 10^{-5}\,\mathrm{K^{-1}}\). What is its length at −10.0°C?
questions.hint: Use \(\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T\) with ΔT negative.

explanations_tiered.definition: Linear expansion formula: ΔL = L₀ α ΔT. When cooled, ΔT is negative and length decreases.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The change in length is proportional to the original length, coefficient of expansion, and temperature change.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When the wire cools, it shrinks. The change is small but measurable.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The formula is linear for small ΔT; for large ΔT, higher-order terms may be needed, but here they are negligible.
misconceptions: Some forget that cooling gives negative ΔT, leading to incorrect addition instead of subtraction.

glossary_entry.definition: Decrease in length when cooled, given by ΔL = L₀ α ΔT (ΔT negative).

flashcard.front: How much does a 5 m steel wire contract when cooled from 20°C to −10°C?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students that ΔT = T_f − T_i can be negative.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve linear expansion problems including contraction

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.3 Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity
questions.question: The specific heat of water is approximately 4186 J/(kg·K), and the specific heat of iron is 448 J/(kg·K). If equal masses of water and iron absorb the same amount of heat, which statement is correct?

options.A: Water temperature increases more than iron
options.B: Iron temperature increases more than water
options.C: Both have the same temperature increase
options.D: Iron absorbs more heat than water

questions.hint: Use ΔT = Q/(mc); smaller c gives larger ΔT for same Q and m.
questions.explanation: For the same mass and heat input, ΔT is inversely proportional to specific heat. Since iron has much lower specific heat than water, its temperature rises more. Water's high specific heat resists temperature change.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.3 Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity
source_trace.content_anchor: specific heat of water is 4186, iron is 448

explanations_tiered.definition: Specific heat capacity is the heat required to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1 K.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Materials with low specific heat (metals) heat up quickly; materials with high specific heat (water) heat up slowly.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Iron heats up faster than water because it takes less heat to raise its temperature.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From ΔT = Q/(mc), if Q and m are the same, ΔT ∝ 1/c. Since c_iron << c_water, ΔT_iron >> ΔT_water.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Water's high specific heat is due to hydrogen bonding, which requires more energy to increase molecular motion compared to metals where electrons and lattice vibrations dominate.
reasoning_steps: Iron has smaller c → larger temperature increase
misconceptions: Some think water always heats faster because it is common; in fact, water heats slowly due to high specific heat.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Low c → heats quickly. High c → heats slowly.

flashcard.front: Which heats faster: iron or water?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Iron, because it has lower specific heat capacity.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which heats more for same Q and m: water or iron?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Iron, because it has lower specific heat capacity (ΔT = Q/(mc)).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Heat to raise 1 kg by 1 K.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Low c → large ΔT. High c → small ΔT.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use the beach example: sand (low c) heats quickly; water (high c) stays cool.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare heating rates using specific heat
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 5.3 Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. For same Q and m, ΔT ∝ 1/c; iron has lower c, so its temperature increase is larger.
